prerequisites: ANTH 2102 or ANTH 2104 , or consent of the department.

description: This course introduces students to the potential of GIS for anthropology. Students will first learn how to operate the basic functions of a leading GIS software. Students will also learn how to create databases that are mapped on all sorts of landscapes.
prerequisites: Any ANTH 1000-level course

course_title: Sociology of Black and African Canadians
description: The course will explore the political, economic, and socio-cultural life of Black and African Canadians from a sociological perspective. Topics that may be covered in this class include the historical and ongoing connections to Africa and the African diaspora, colonialism, immigration, racism, sports, education, and employment.
prerequisites: SLGY 1101 or consent of the department.

description: This course will examine the contemporary migration and im(mobility) of people across borders. Central to this course will be an understanding of how colonialism, racism, and sexism intersect with migration, identity politics, state policies, and international governance of migration.
prerequisites: One 2000-level sociology course

description: Under the guidance of a faculty supervisor the student will work on a research project in the area of biology. A proposal will be presented to a supervisory committee.
prerequisites: Admission into the Biology Honours program

description: Under the guidance of a faculty supervisor, the student will continue to work on a research project in the area of biology. The project results will be presented to a supervisory committee.
prerequisites: BIOL 5501

course_title: Data Science I: Introduction to Databases
description: This course emphasizes concepts necessary to design and implement databases using a variety of data models including relational, and non-relational models. Practical application with a number of Database Management Systems is included with an emphasis on data processing in a data science environment.
prerequisites: A grade of C- or higher in either COMP 1701 or COMP 2001 .

description: This course critically introduces the relevance of peacebuilding, exploring how we can transcend conflict that impacts our lives and human community while still living within it. It enhances and interrogates our understanding of the relationship between peace and justice through critical analysis of personal, historical, social, political, cultural and economic factors. Students will gain and use essential skills, tools and opportunities for sustained peacebuilding. The specific content of the course will vary by term and instructor. Prerequisite(s) / Corequisite(s): GNED 1301 or GNED 1303 .

This course provides an introductory overview of the main sources and types of law in Canada and how laws are made. The course addresses the imposition of colonial forms of law over Indigenous law in Canada. The major types of public and private law in Canada, the meaning of legal reasoning, and legal liability are explored.

This dialogue-based course gives students an opportunity to assess the interlocking nature of the forces of social domination and resistance such as those based on social class, nationalism, race, gender, sexuality, religion, and politics. The class will investigate together these intersections in social life through critical reading and writing and through engagement in the process of individual and collective reflection on both our inner and outer worlds and experiences.
prerequisites: Any sociology course at the 3000-level.

description: Success in international trade means that your product must get into the hands of prospective customers in the most efficient, safe and cost-effective manner possible. Topics in this course include distribution channels, partnering, barriers to entry, market entry strategies, managing a joint venture, investment-based strategies and exit strategies.
prerequisites: INBU 3301 and INBU 3302

course_title: Analytical Biochemistry in a Community Context
description: In this course, students will apply their training in research and chemistry to assist community partner(s) to gain valuable insights toward addressing their unique question(s). Working closely with the partner(s), students will develop and execute a research program and report findings. Through involvement with the community partner(s), students will gain real-world experience and understand the relevance of chemistry in the world around them. This course is the culmination of the community service learning component of the Analytical Biochemistry concentration in the Chemistry B.Sc.
prerequisites: BCEM 3202 , CHEM 3200 and CHEM 4301 all with a grade of C- or better Community Service Learning

course_title: Practicing Public Sociology - Work Integrated Learning
description: This course offers a critical study of public and applied sociology with a focus on how to apply sociological skills and knowledge beyond the university. Central to this course will be an opportunity to apply sociological skills in a work-integrated learning context such as with government departments or agencies, not-for-profit organizations, Indigenous governments and organizations, or private sector companies. Possible topics may include the application of sociology in migration, globalization, Indigenous rights, human rights, health, disability, environment, data and policy analysis, social justice, or social services.
prerequisites: SLGY 2232 or consent of the department.

description: In this course, students will apply their training in research and chemistry to assist community partner(s) to gain problem-solving insights. Working closely with the community partner(s), students will develop and execute a research program and report findings. Through involvement with the community partner(s), students will gain real-world experience and understand the relevance of chemistry in the world around them. This course is the culmination of the community service learning component of the General Chemistry concentration in the Chemistry B.Sc.
prerequisites: CHEM 3200 and CHEM 3202 with a minimum grade of C-. Community Service Learning

description: This course provides an introduction to multiple linear regression and time series analysis. Relationship between a single response variable and several explanatory variables will be modelled via regression models. Main topics include multiple linear regression, logistic regression, quantile regression and nonlinear regression. Practical aspects of these regression models with applications to problems in Data Science with focus on interpretation, estimation, and hypothesis testing of the parameters of the respective models will be presented.
prerequisites: MATH 2444 with a minimum grade of C-. General Science Option

description: This course will provide students with an introduction to the theoretical and philosophical bases of qualitative inquiry in Psychology as well as familiarize students with the basic skills needed to conduct a qualitative study. Topics included in this course may include qualitative research designs, methods of data collection, issues of scientific rigour, ethical dilemmas, and data analysis.
prerequisites: PSYC 2213 with a minimum grade of C or higher.

description: This course will focus on the development of career-readiness skills within the discipline of Psychology by allowing students to observe how the psychological concepts learned across the degree program transfer to the workplace. Students will be assisted in finding an approved work-term placement with a community partner that deals with matters directly related to an area of psychology. Depending on the work placement, appropriate police record and other checks may be required (e.g., Police Information Check, Vulnerable Sector Check, Intervention Record Check).
prerequisites: A minimum grade of B in both PSYC 2213 and in one 3000-level Psychology course

course_title: From Factory to Gig: A World of Workers
description: Anthropology examines how people make a living and how they organize their lives around activities that generate income. Building on the anthropology of work and finance, this course explores the emergence of the worker in the capitalist system and traces various social relations that shape workers’ lives and work alternatives.
prerequisites: Any 1000-level course in Anthropology.

description: In this course, students will explore the role that forensic anthropologists play in medico-legal investigations, mass disasters, and human rights cases. Students will learn how to identify individualizing features and build a biological profile from skeletonized remains. The course will also examine topics such as forensic archaeology, time since death, taphonomy, and trauma.
prerequisites: Any 1000-Level ANTH course.

description: This course explores different eras of Indigenous Maya civilization, from its inception 3,000 years ago to its disruption by the Spanish invasion in the 16th century. Through the lens of archaeology and anthropology, students will learn about many aspects of Maya Civilization, such as architecture, trade routes, religion, language, governments, and key figures of ancient Maya history.
prerequisites: ANTH 1105

description: Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.
prerequisites: Consent of the department. General Science Option

description: Credit(s): 3 Other Hours Schedule Type: Field Work 3 week field school This course is designed to provide students with a learning experience outside of the traditional classroom setting. Students will travel locally, nationally or internationally to a specific and relevant location connected with Indigenous land-relational learning. Coinciding with relational learning and Indigenous perspectives, students will learn the connection to this knowledge to educational theories. Students will also develop practical and experiential connections with theory throughout the course. Please note that additional fees (travel, accommodation, sustenance, program fees) will apply outside of the standard tuition. Prerequisite(s): One of GNED 1301 or GNED 1303 and consent of the department. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
prerequisites: One of GNED 1301 or GNED 1303 and consent of the department.

course_title: Presenting Complex Ideas Through Narration and Visual Media
description: Credit(s): 3 Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture A well-told story is a means of discovery . While multiple disciplines focus on the translation of information using visual media, this course seeks to deepen speakers ’ understanding of the unique tools necessary for effective and impactful oral communication of complex concepts. The oral modality creates unique demands for presenters with regard to structure, visual representation and delivery of ideas. Students will learn practical skills to create compelling visual narratives to communicate challenging content to lay audiences. Prerequisite(s): SPCH 2001 GNED Cluster 4 - Tier 3
prerequisites: SPCH 2001

description: This course reinforces the programming knowledge and introduces basic data structures and algorithms useful for data analysis. Students also learn important programming tools and frameworks for data analysis.
prerequisites: MATH 1203 and COMP 1701 , each with a minimum grade of C-. General Science Option

description: Victimology is a multi-disciplinary study of victims of crime, the impact of crime on their lives, and the criminal justice system’s response to crime and victimisation. It draws insight from sociology, psychological, criminology, and other disciplines to understand the social-structural causes and consequences of victimisation, the intersecting individual and structural factors that promote victimisation of specific people and groups, and the criminal justice system’s response to victimisation.
prerequisites: Either both CRJS 1001 AND CRJS 1003; OR SLGY 1101

description: This course is an introduction to physical literacy. This course provides foundational knowledge of the physical, cognitive, social and emotional domains of physical literacy. The construct of physical literacy will be explored across the sectors of sport, recreation and education.
prerequisites: HPED 1040 , and one of HPED 1000 , EDUC 1231 or EDUC 1233 .

description: Under the guidance of a faculty supervisor, the student designs and conducts work on a research project in the area of geology. A proposal, which includes preliminary data and/or results, is presented as an oral presentation and as a written submission to a supervisory committee.
prerequisites: Permission of the department

description: Under the guidance of a faculty supervisor, the student carries out the proposed research project planned in GEOL 5501 (Honours Research I), which includes the collection, analysis, and interpretation of data. The project results are presented as a manuscript and as an oral or poster presentation to a scientific audience. The student also defends their results and conclusions during a formal session with their supervisory committee.
prerequisites: GEOL 5501 , with a minimum grade of C-.

description: This course introduces topics from discrete mathematics and probability that are important in the study of data science. Topics include logic, relations, combinatorics, graph theory, and Bayesian probability. Monte Carlo simulations are also introduced and implemented through the use of relevant programming languages.
prerequisites: One of MATH 2233 , MATH 2234 , MATH 2235 , MATH 2333 , MGMT 2262 or ENVS 1105 , and both MATH 1200 and MATH 1203 , each with a grade of C- or higher. General Science Option

course_title: Advanced Topics in Infant and Child Social Development
description: This course provides an advanced investigation into psychological research with regard to social development throughout infancy and childhood. Topics may include the role of play in development, emotional development, gender development, temperament, attachment, prosocial development, aggression, parenting, friendships, or other current topics related to social development in infancy and childhood. Regardless of the specific topics covered, this course will examine theoretical and empirical aspects of social development as well as applications to education and/or parenting practices.
prerequisites: A minimum grade of C in PSYC 2213 and PSYC 3351 , or consent of the department.

description: This is the first course in the Student Investment Fund experience (hereafter, the “SIF”). The SIF is an experiential exercise in the Investment Fund Management (IFM) concentration of the BBA - Finance Program. This is a required course in IFM concentration. The course enhances class- and lecture-based learning with hands-on experience in economic and industry analysis, including valuation overviews for all the stocks in their industry sector(s). They will also have to present a stock pitch, which must include a robust and defensible financial model, including target price, to report in a classic investment text.
prerequisites: A minimum grade of B- in FNCE 4407 and FNCE 4409 .

description: FNCE 4502 is the second required course of the Student Investment Fund experience (hereafter, the “SIF”). The SIF is an experiential exercise for Finance and Accounting students. The course enhances class- and lecture-based learning with hands-on experience in portfolio management and performance reporting, including equity execution in the live market. In FNCE 4502, students prepare and present a portfolio performance report and a performance attribution report.
prerequisites: FNCE 4501

course_title: Special Topics in Ecology and Evolution
description: This course explores a special topic in ecology and evolution that is not currently offered within the existing Biology curriculum. Students are presented with an advanced topic and learn new content or expand on foundational material from previous courses. Each topic is announced and advertised by the Department in advance, as different topics may be covered each time the course is offered.
prerequisites: BIOL 3401 and one of BIOL 3106 , BIOL 3108 or BIOL 3301 , each with a grade of C- or higher.

course_title: Work Integrated Learning in General Science
description: Participate in a work-integrated learning experience applying theoretical knowledge and practical skills in a workplace environment of a local organization. Investigate a science-related issue identified by the organization and produce a deliverable for this project. The specific deliverable varies based on the nature of the placement, the academic background of the student, and the needs of the organization. Specific projects could involve collection, sorting, or analysis of information; summarizing or interpreting information; or using problem-solving skills to interrogate an issue.
prerequisites: SCIE 5010 and consent of the department.

description: The aim of exercise psychology is to understand psychological influences behind exercise behaviour and to encourage healthy choices. In this course students will identify barriers to and opportunities for healthy behaviour from a psycho-social lens. Students will learn behaviour change theories to improve, reinforce and adhere to healthy behaviour.
prerequisites: HPED 1000, HPED 1040 and HPED 2030

description: This course is designed to provide students with a learning experience outside of the traditional classroom setting. Students will travel nationally or internationally to a discipline-specific and relevant location related to their program of study. Students will apply theoretical principles learned throughout the program. A project (typically research-based) will be completed while on location or data collection will occur throughout the experience. Please note that additional fees (travel, accommodation, sustenance, and program fees) will apply outside of the standard tuition.
prerequisites: Approval of the department.

course_title: Historical Foundations of Recreation, Health, Sport, Physical Literacy and the Outdoors
description: In this course, students will explore the historical foundations of recreation, health, sport, physical literacy, and the outdoors as it relates to historical methods and processes such as colonialism, capitalism, and patriarchy.
prerequisites: HPED 1040

description: This course provides an introduction to the concepts, methods and tools of logical, quantitative, and qualitative analyses to business decision-making and problem-solving under conditions of certainty, uncertainty, risk and competition. Both single person and group decision making are covered and applied problems from many areas of managerial decision making are considered. The course also covers topics such as behavioural economics, risk management and project decision-making, designed to explore the interpretation of results and the strengths and limitations of the various tools.
prerequisites: MGMT 2262 or MATH 2234

course_title: Medicine, Science, & Empire in South Asia c. 1750-1930
description: Although scientific and medical knowledge are considered universal, this course investigates how the political, economic, and social contexts of European empires affect what is considered authoritative scientific thought and practice. Drawing conceptually from the historiography of subaltern studies and post-colonial theory, the course shows how ideologies of empire informed both the priorities of scientific investigation as well as their narration. The course focuses specifically on the British Empire in South Asia.
prerequisites: HIST 2202

course_title: Special Topics in Supply Chain Management
description: This course provides an examination of selected topics in Supply Chain Management. As this is a variable content course, the specific topic will be announced and advertised each time the course is offered.
prerequisites: LSCM 2201

description: This course will provide an overview of trauma psychology, including historical perspectives, the evolving definition of psychological trauma, research methodologies, and diagnosis and treatment of trauma-related psychological disorders. Other topics discussed will include various trauma-related contexts, as well as physiological, cognitive, emotional and social impacts of psychological trauma on individuals and groups. Cultural components of trauma, post-traumatic growth and resilience will also be explored.
prerequisites: A minimum grade of C in all of PSYC 2213 , PSYC 2285 and one 3000-level PSYC course.

description: Credit(s): 3 Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course draws upon the developments and insights within the field of Indigenous Studies to offer students the critical tools to unpack the ways in which Indigenous scholars have thought through, criticized, and theorized the operation of colonial power. Students will engage with Indigenous ontologies and epistemologies and network them with critical scholarship that diagnoses the foreclosure of Indigenous ways of relating in a colonial world. Prerequisite(s): INST 1101
prerequisites: INST 1101

description: Metabolism is central to maintaining life. This course provides an overview of human metabolism from biochemistry to disease with topics ranging from basic biochemical pathways, cellular aspects of metabolic regulation, and diseases related to metabolic dysfunction. Lectures focus on establishing a strong knowledge base in metabolic concepts while the research project provides students the opportunity to use applied metabolic measurements in course-based undergraduate research.
prerequisites: BIOL 2110 , BIOL 2202 , BCEM 2201 and BIOL 3401 , each with a grade of C- or higher. General Science Option

description: This course provides the student with a solid foundation in multiple and complex facets involved in airline management. Subjects will focus on the increasing use of data analytics in determining airline strategic decisions involved in route demand analysis, scheduling optimization, competitive slot times, fleet planning, aircraft leasing, maintenance scheduling and fleet modernization decision-making. The course will further develop the student’s skills in analyzing and creating profit plans in both passenger and cargo departments. Human resources, crew scheduling, executive management, shareholder and board reporting, and regulatory compliance and safety issues within management will be further discussed. Students will be introduced to the increasing pressure on airlines to limit carbon emissions through technological advancements and route structure design. A strong focus will be placed on the student’s understanding of managing safety management and fatigue risk management systems as well as thoroughly understanding flight dispatch operations. Practical time spent in an Operational Dispatch Department and in tracking SMS/FRMS data to implement operational management changes will be undertaken.
prerequisites: AIRF 2111 , HRES 2170 and MGMT 2130

description: This course will prepare students to undertake airport operations and management roles. Students will learn how airports are planned and developed and how revenue is planned and created through an in-depth understanding of each role and department within airport operations. Students will further understand labour relations, air transport industry negotiations, airport marketing, cargo and logistics hubs, and safety operations, as well as environmental, social, and governance issues related to airport management. AVSEC (Aviation Security Systems and Measures) and SMS systems will be reviewed.
prerequisites: AIRF 2111 , HRES 2170 and MGMT 2130

description: This course will give the student a more in-depth understanding of the economic impact of airlines and airports on the global economy, airline pricing and revenues, airline and airport corporate strategic financial planning, the international regulatory framework, the impact of deregulation, the geo-politics of aviation, airport economics and its integration with airlines, the environmental issues, global airport financial models, and challenges facing the air transport industry. The course will further review the roles of IATA, TIACA, ICAO, and various labour unions and management relations that affect air transport economics. The impact of key global disruptions such as Covid-19, airspace closures, and other geo-political events on air transport economic models will be reviewed in detail to build data-informed models and to seek risk mitigation strategies to limit financial damage to the airline and airport sectors and to the regional and global economies.
prerequisites: AIRF 4201 , AIRF 4202 , and LSCM 3403

description: This course will give students a more in-depth understanding of the economic impact of airlines on the global economy, airline pricing and revenues, airline corporate strategic financial planning, the international regulatory framework, the impact of deregulation, the geopolitics of aviation, airport economics and its integration with airlines, the environmental issues, global airport financial models, and challenges facing the air transport industry. The course will further review the roles of IATA, TIACA, ICAO, and various labour unions and management relations that affect airline economics. The impact of key geo-political events such as Covid-19 on airline economic models and future geo-political challenges will be reviewed in detail to build robust models and to seek risk mitigation strategies to limit financial damage to the airline and to the regional and global economies.
prerequisites: AIRF 4201 and AIRF 4202

course_title: Life Cycle Analysis - Aviation Systems and Programs
description: This course will introduce students to aircraft and component life cycle analysis and its impact on effective airline operations and revenues. Differences in life cycle analysis and aviation systems and management programs between regional and national international carriers will be reviewed. Students will understand the market in engine leasing and component life cycle acquisition and contracts. Students will have a strong understanding of the global MRO industry and aircraft maintenance cycles and the importance of timing in parts turnover, overhaul, and planning removal from service. Real-world visits to review and analyze the maintenance systems of actual operating airlines will form part of the program.
prerequisites: AIRF 4201 and AIRF 4202

description: This course prepares students to execute strategic management decisions within the airline environment. Case studies will be used to review issues faced by management in the aviation industry in making strategic decisions to guide the airline to growth and profitability. Timing for fleet expansion, new route structures, joint agreements and alliances will be reviewed. STAR, SkyTeam and OneWorld will be researched.
prerequisites: AIRF 4203 , AIRF 4204 , and AIRF 4205

course_title: Aviation Environmental Social Governance and Corporate Ethics
description: This course will give students a sound foundation in the environmental, social, and governance issues affecting the global aviation industry. The aviation industry’s attempts to lower carbon emissions through technological advancements, alternative fuels, optimized and integrated next-generation air traffic control, and automation will be reviewed. Global carbon credit mechanisms will be reviewed, as well as various marketing initiatives and mechanisms the industry is employing. Challenges to the aviation industry will also be reviewed, including pressure to reduce mass tourism, and future aviation business models will be analyzed.
prerequisites: AIRF 4203 , AIRF 4204 , and AIRF 4205

course_title: Physical Activity and the Older Adult
description: This course will focus on topics related to lifespan development and the issues pertaining to participation in physical activity for older adults. There will be an emphasis on experential learning and applied exercise leadership including assessment and programming.
prerequisites: PHYL 3518 and a Police Information Check with a Vulnerable Sector Check completed within 10 months of the course start date is required for participation in this course.

course_title: Nationalism and Empire in South Asia
description: Credit(s): 3 Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course offers an introduction to the study of modern South Asia, a region of more than 1.7 billion people, with particular attention to the political, social, and economic forces that have shaped the history of the sub-continent from the 18th to the 20th century. It asks: how does anti-colonial nationalism differ from European nationalism? How have South Asian political leaders created global solidarities along categories of caste, race, and religion to forge new polities in the region? Students will explore hierarchies associated with modern India, most significantly those connected to religion, the politics of language, and caste/untouchability. Students will be introduced to the work of political thinkers and artists, who may include Mohandas Gandhi, Muhammad Ali Jinnah, B.R. Ambedkar, Jawaharlal Nehru, Amrita Sher-Gill and Khushwant Singh.

course_title: Special Topics in Cellular and Molecular Biology
description: Credit(s): 3 Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course allows an exploration of special topics in cellular and molecular biology. Students are presented with an advanced topic that is not currently offered within the existing Biology curriculum. As topics will vary from semester to semester, topics will be announced and advertised by the Department each time the course is offered. A maximum of two Special Topics courses can be used towards graduation. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 3101 , BIOL 3102 and BIOL 3401 , each with a grade of C- or higher. Note: This course cannot be used as a General Science Option. A maximum of two Special Topics courses can be used towards graduation.
prerequisites: BIOL 3101 , BIOL 3102 and BIOL 3401 , each with a grade of C- or higher.

description: This course explores the social, cultural and political changes within early-modern South Asia. This course focuses on the mobility that characterized intra-Asia networks of trade, plunder, knowledge acquisition, and the growth of new cities. It also balances this global history approach with an attention to local studies of culture and cultural change.

This course explores foodways through the scientific study of archaeological food remains. Using a multidisciplinary approach, students learn how humans adapted to their environment by gathering, hunting, raising, and growing food in the past. Topics include past human diets, systems of food production, and the role of sociopolitics and economics.
prerequisites: Any 1000-level course in Anthropology

description: This course introduces students to classic and contemporary theories associated with interpersonal violence, gang violence, state-sponsored violence, extra-state violence, and violence associated with transnational organized crime. Students will first gain a foundational understanding of the relevant theories associated with violence at an interpersonal level, group level, and transnational level. Issues covered will include interpersonal level, group violence, state-sponsored violence, and conclude with non-state-sponsored (extra-state) violence. Societal responses to violence will also be examined throughout the course.
prerequisites: CRJS 1001 or SLGY 1101

description: In today’s rapidly evolving scientific landscape, the ability to communicate research findings, hypotheses, and complex biological concepts clearly and persuasively is crucial. Whether aspiring biologists plan to pursue careers in academia, research, industry, or public policy, this course provides the tools and strategies foundational to their chosen career path. Starting with foundational concepts in scientific writing and communication, this course then delves deeper into a range of ways to tell scientific stories, with an emphasis on effective communication to a variety of audiences.
prerequisites: Third year standing (60 credits) in the Biology Major Program

description: This course takes a critical approach to examining health and healthcare access issues facing 2SLGBTQI+ communities. Students will problematize narratives of vulnerability and risk, and examine narratives of “health” itself. This course will offer an intersectional and transnational perspective to explore unique issues faced by 2SLGBTQI+ people historically and over the life course. Students will gain foundational knowledge and nuanced understanding of key issues and approaches to 2SLGBTQIA+ health by engaging with emerging scholarship and examples of collective movements for social change.

course_title: History of Women and Gender in Islamic Societies
description: This course provides an examination of the historical narratives and discourses pertaining to women and gender in the Islamic world. Central to the course is the analysis of both the enduring aspects and evolving dimensions of women’s status, the construction of gender, and discussions concerning sexuality and the body as they are informed and influenced by the complex interplay of religious, cultural, legal, economic, medical, and political discourses and realities within the Islamic world. Topics might include the lived experiences of women in the context of their political, religious, economic, and socio-legal circumstances.

course_title: Professional Nursing Skills: Consolidated lab experience
description: Credit(s): 1 Lab Hour(s): 32 This course provides opportunity for Internationally Educated Nurses (IENs) to practice and apply the theory and clinical judgement skills needed to complete a holistic health assessment. IENs will utilize a health promotion approach to conduct interviews, complete a health history, conduct physical examinations with the purpose of identifying, prioritizing, and managing alterations in health. This course provides opportunity for IENs to practice and demonstrate competence performing nursing skills within the context of simulated patient care case studies. Nursing skills include assessment, clinical judgement, psychomotor skills, math for medications, relationship building, communication, collaboration, and documentation.

Credit(s): 1.5 Lecture Hour(s): 1.5 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture An introduction to the facilitation and programming strategies that are used to promote physical activity and mental wellness in an outdoor environment for individuals experiencing disability. The course will focus on applying theory to real-life settings and familiarization with specialized equipment and techniques. This course will explore the philosophical underpinnings of identifying and removing barriers to empower individuals to access the outdoor environment.
prerequisites: One of EDUC 1233 , HPED 1010 or HPED 2010

description: This course is developed and delivered by Old Sun Community College, in partnership with MRU, and is tailored to meet the needs of Siksika Nation and individuals reflecting the Siksika Way of Life. There is a vast amount of literature on Western Leadership, however, there are limited books available on Indigenous Leadership and the interest of studying Indigenous Leadership is growing. Western and Indigenous leadership differ. In general, western theories teach becoming a good leader with a focus on individual achievement, concerned with the results and achieving goals rather than relationship. Historically and today, Indigenous leadership focuses on community or collectivism, with an emphasis on the responsibility to take care of the people and the community. This course will prepare students to gain essential knowledge and skills to equip them in working for Indigenous organizations. By understanding Indigenous Leadership, students will be prepared to help lead Indigenous organizations towards making meaningful contributions to support and better Indigenous communities.

course_title: Special Topics in Indigenous Treaty Law
description: This course is developed and delivered by Old Sun Community College, in partnership with MRU, and is tailored to meet the needs of Siksika Nation and individuals reflecting the Siksika Way of Life. Students will explore and examine both the historic and contemporary First Nations - Crown treaties involving the European settler society (French and English). Students will examine both historic and contemporary treaties from a constitutional perspective involving section 35 of the Constitution Act, 1982 , including current case law dealing with treaty and aboriginal rights. The significance of the Royal Proclamation of 1763 , and the Rupert’s Land and North-Western Order of 1870 and its equitable principles doctrine will be examined. Finally, First Nation - Crown treaty relations will be examined from an economic reconciliation perspective taking into account of recent developments in greater government, private industry and First Nation economic partnerships directly as a result of Supreme Court of Canada decisions favouring First Nations and international developments such as the United Nations Declaration on the Rights Peoples of Indigenous Peoples (2007).

Credit(s): 1.5 Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is designed to provide students with the theory and practice of fly fishing, including its philosophical underpinnings, techniques, etiquette, and connections to societal health and wellbeing. This course emphasizes self awareness, sustainability and environmental stewardship as key pillars of this activity. This course is divided between preparatory lectures and off-campus activities.

course_title: Social and Emotional Domains of Physical Literacy
description: This course explores the social and emotional domains of physical literacy as determinants of well-being across diverse sociocultural contexts. Students will critically explore how belonging, motivation, confidence, and cultural identity shape engagement and contribute to human flourishing within diverse communities. Through applied learning and awareness, students will integrate cultural competencies and investigate inclusive practices to value the importance of social connection and emotional well-being of physical literacy.
prerequisites: HPED 3010 and PHYL 3510

description: This course brings philosophy together with critical health studies, exploring a range of philosophical issues related to power, ethics, somatic practices, authority, and embodied experiences. Areas of philosophy will include philosophy of disability, as well as transdisciplinary areas like critical disability studies.

This course offers an in-depth exploration of current trends and key debates in management. The theme of the special topics course varies with each offering but will focus on a specific topic that is relevant to current business trends and issues. Topics may include shifting industry priorities, leadership, working within the broader social and environmental contexts, macroeconomic trends, firm-level issues, and team dynamics. Students will gain a comprehensive understanding of the selected topic through a combination of theoretical frameworks, practical applications, case studies, and discussions. As this is a variable content course, the specific topic will be announced each time the course is offered.

This is a credit-free upgrading course; special fees apply. This course comprehensively covers the essential topics of algebra and the basic principles of geometry to an intermediate level. The course is designed to meet the needs of students who have a minimal background in algebra or whose proficiency may have declined during years away from formal education. Successful completion of this course prepares students for MATH 0115, where algebraic skills are utilized to study more advanced topics.

course_title: Analytical Chemistry II: Introduction to Instrumental Analysis
description: This course follows CHEM 2301 (Analytical Chemistry 1, Quantitative Analysis) and introduces students to the principles and practice of instrumental methods in quantitative analysis. Lectures will cover spectroscopic analysis (UV-visible, atomic absorption/emission), electroanalytical methods (potentiometry, voltametry, coulometry and redox titrations) and analytical separation methods (extraction methods, chromatography [including ICP, HPLC, GC, GC-MS] and capillary electrophoresis). Labs will cover quantitative analysis of organic and inorganic materials using some of the above methods.
prerequisites: CHEM 2301 and CHEM 3200 with a grade of C-.

description: This course introduces techniques for ethically and responsibly wrangling and manipulating datasets to make them appropriate for addressing the question at hand. Topics may include cleaning and transforming data, integrity and quality measures, common file formats, feature selection and engineering, and generating features from unstructured sources such as text and images.
prerequisites: DATA 2402 and MATH 2444 , each with a minimum grade of C-.

description: This course introduces principles of visual design, perceptual psychology, and cognitive science to guide students in creating clear and effective visualizations of complex data. Students will learn charting and data representation techniques using a variety of software tools. The goal is to equip students with the skills to produce sophisticated visualizations that clearly communicate insights in data.
prerequisites: DATA 2402 with minimum grade of C-.

description: This course introduces techniques for obtaining data from databases, documents, the web, and other sources. Further topics include concepts of data quality and how to address quality issues. Data science process models, the critical role of data acquisition and labeling within the larger business process, and ethical considerations in collecting data are all emphasized.
prerequisites: DATA 2721 and DATA 2402 each with a minimum grade of C-.

description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of accounting information systems (AIS) and their role in modern accounting practices. Students will explore the interplay between accounting processes and information technology, with a focus on risk management, internal controls, software systems, and data analysis. Key topics include risk assessment, system design, and emerging technologies, with practical hands-on experience using QuickBooks software. By the end of the course, students will be proficient in managing accounting systems and leveraging data for strategic decision-making.
prerequisites: ACCT 3221 and MGMT 2262

description: This course examines the role of international law, tribunals, and criminal courts in the prevention and prosecution of international crime and human rights violations. Topics will include the theoretical foundations of international law and human rights, as well as substantive cases of human rights violations and their prosecution.
prerequisites: CRJS 1001 and CRJS 2015

description: This course builds on skills related to audio and video production acquired in the first year of the Broadcast Media Studies program. Students will become proficient in camera operation, video editing, and audio recording. The course will focus on technical skills so students are ready for independent production projects. These transversal skills will position students well for subsequent courses in the program and beyond graduation.
prerequisites: BMST 1404 with a minimum grade of C or higher.

description: This is an interactive seminar and project-based course in which students will explore the themes of critical appraisal of scientific literature, scientific integrity and professionalism, and scientific communication. Students will be given the opportunity for in-depth independent investigation on a topic that integrates across sub-disciplines in Biology.
prerequisites: A grade of C- or higher in BIOL 3401 and any two 4000-level Biology Approved Options.

description: This course explores current events and issues in terms of the impact they may have on development. Topics covered will vary from one semester to the next; these might include the effects of epidemics/pandemics on psychosocial development, or the effects of the current economic climate on postsecondary students’ self-concept and motivation, or current research on disorders in childhood, or the causes and effects of cyberbullying, or the causes and effects of elder abuse, or topics regarding other events that are current in a given semester.
prerequisites: PSYC 2213 and PSYC 2235 , and one 3000-level PSYC course, all with a minimum grade of C.

course_title: The Psychology of Parenting: Work Integrated Learning
description: This course examines the psychological theory and research behind parenting and parent-child relationships. Students will examine parenting through the lens of developmental, psychological, clinical, and contextual factors, with a focus on how parent-child interactions shape behaviour and outcomes. Topics include parenting styles, discipline, attachment, cultural influences, and the impact of parenting on children’s development from infancy through adolescence. The course emphasizes a reciprocal relationship between parents and children, highlighting how both shape and influence each other’s growth, with particular attention to insights from clinical psychology on parent-child interactions and family dynamics. As part of this course, students will be paired with a community partner to complete a work-integrated learning experience.
prerequisites: PSYC 2213 with a minimum grade of C.

description: Honours students explore the synthesis of research and theory within the study of Journalism and Digital Media, Broadcast Media Studies, Public Relations, or Information Design. Students develop a literature review and research proposal in preparation for the Communications Honours Research Project (COMM 5020) in the Winter semester. Discussions will also focus on preparation for graduate schools and professional schools. Emphasis is placed on students’ ability to think critically, ethically, and independently.
prerequisites: Final grade of B+ or higher in either COMM 3500 and COMM 3738 , INFO 3600 and INFO 3605 , or PUBR 3855 or PUBR 3860 .

description: Under the one-on-one supervision of a full-time faculty member, COMMs Honours students will conduct an in-depth academic investigation of a selected topic in their discipline, giving them the opportunity to integrate theory and practice and further their expertise in their field. Students will spend the semester writing, editing, and rewriting their draft thesis. Data collection and analysis (if relevant) will also take place. Expectation is that students will submit a completed Honours thesis at the end of the academic year (April).
prerequisites: COMM 5010

description: This course is the second of two thesis courses that will provide a more extensive, year-long exposure to the knowledge and practice of the discipline. The student will be expected to design and complete a project of their choosing in an area of health and physical education. In Honours Thesis II, the thesis will be presented as a paper and an oral presentation to an academic committee. The Honours thesis project is conducted under the direct guidance of a faculty supervisor from the Department of Health and Physical Education. In exceptional circumstances, a co-supervisor from outside of the department may be named.
prerequisites: HPED 5000

course_title: Determinants of Addictions - Work Integrated Learning
description: This course introduces the history, methods, and current state of knowledge in selected areas of modern addictions research. A range of content areas will be surveyed, including idiosyncratic influences at the genetic, cellular, anatomical, cognitive-behavioral, and sociocultural levels that can impact the susceptibility to substance use disorders, substance use/abuse, and behavioral addictions. As part of this course, students will be paired with a community partner to complete a work-integrated learning experience.
prerequisites: PSYC 2213 with a grade of C or higher.

description: Artificial Intelligence for Business is a hands-on, innovative course that introduces students to practical AI applications within the business world. This course empowers students to leverage AI tools to tackle real-world business challenges and explore the transformative potential of AI in various domains. Through interactive learning and mini-projects, students gain essential skills in data analysis, machine learning, AI-driven innovation, and ethical AI use. The course offers a “technical explanation for the non-technical” approach, which is accessible to all students, regardless of prior AI experience​.

This is a mentored project-based course on data and questions from a partner within or outside the university. Students may choose projects related to the concentration that they have chosen and work with a mentor with expertise in that domain. With consultation with their mentors, students will formulate questions and design and execute a suitable solution. Students will produce a project report, presentation, and possibly other products, such as a computer application to demonstrate the solution.
prerequisites: DATA 3453 and DATA 3464 both with a minimum grade of C- and department approval.

description: This course introduces students to the foundational concepts and tools of bioinformatics. Students will gain practical experience with bioinformatics software, databases, and algorithms and learn to work with large datasets, perform sequence comparisons and searches, and analyze DNA, RNA, and protein sequences using various bioinformatics tools. By the end of the course, students will be able to implement bioinformatics pipelines, design computational experiments, and critically evaluate bioinformatics research.
prerequisites: BIOL 2101 and one of: MATH 2233 , MATH 2234 , MATH 2235 or MATH 2333 , each with a minimum grade of C- or higher

description: This course offers an introduction to Network Science, emphasizing the theoretical foundations and practical methodologies for modeling and analyzing complex systems. Key topics include network metrics, algorithms for topological evolution, computational techniques for structural analysis, simulation of dynamics in networks, and machine learning-driven applications for robustness assessment and graph embedding.
prerequisites: MATH 2234 ; and one of MATH 1271 or MATH 2071 , and one of COMP 2631 or DATA 2402 , all with a minimum grade of C-.

course_title: Special Topics in Anatomy and Physiology
description: Credit(s): 3 Lecture Hour(s): 3 This course allows an exploration of special topics in anatomy and physiology. Students are presented with an advanced topic that is not currently offered within the existing Biology curriculum. As topics will vary from semester to semester, topics will be announced and advertised by the department each time the course is offered. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 3401 and one of BIOL 3104 , BIOL 3204 , or BIOL 3205 , each with a minimum grade of C- or higher Note: A maximum of two BIOL special topics courses can be used towards graduation. Back to Top | Print-Friendly Page (opens a new window)
prerequisites: BIOL 3401 and one of BIOL 3104 , BIOL 3204 , or BIOL 3205 , each with a minimum grade of C- or higher

course_title: Building Communities in a Changing World
description: As our world rapidly changes, we need to rethink and reconsider what constitutes healthy, resilient and just communities and societies. In this course, students will interrogate contemporary societal practices and norms in the face of broader challenges to identify community building needs and solutions. Students will critically evaluate theories and practices of social justice for community building and development.

This course will introduce Indigenous science as understood and expressed through Indigenous knowledge systems. Engaging with teachings shared by Elders, knowledge keepers, scientists, and other readings, students will explore Indigenous perspectives of science and its global influences. Prerequisite(s): INST 1101 GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 3 Back to Top | Print-Friendly Page (opens a new window)
prerequisites: INST 1101

description: This course explores the integration of Indigenous knowledge in science with a focus on scientific studies that incorporate First Nations, Metis and Inuit knowledge. Students will examine the intersection between traditional wisdom and current scientific paradigms and evaluate the contributions of Indigenous knowledge to various disciplines of science.
prerequisites: GNED 1101 or GNED 1103 General Science Option,

description: Data, data and more data! This course focuses on data gathering, data management, and data governance within the context of business analytics. The course prepares students to uncover insights from structured and unstructured data sets to inform data-driven decision-making. At the end of the course, students will be able to work through the data life cycle, including pulling relevant data from multiple sources, organising the data, and using information technology to gather and interpret data. Students will also understand the fundamentals of ethical data governance and how to apply them in practice. They will have experience with relational databases and visual analytics.
prerequisites: MGMT 2262

course_title: Introduction to Peace and Justice Studies
description: This course introduces students to Peace and Justice Studies. It offers a conceptual overview of the field in various national, regional, and global contexts. Students will learn about colonialism, settler colonialism, imperialism, civil wars, and inter-state wars and conflicts, with a focus on structural violence and how that poses a major challenge to achieving peace and justice. The course material will explore past and ongoing cases of structural violence across the globe by foregrounding and centralizing the quest for anti-racist, anti-imperial, and decolonial peace and justice in their learning. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 2 Back to Top | Print-Friendly Page (opens a new window)

Credit(s): 3 Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course focuses on developing essential skills in analyzing, interpreting, integrating, and communicating scientific information. Students will learn to critically evaluate current scientific literature from across different disciplines, identify and contextualize key findings, and communicate complex ideas clearly and effectively in various formats, including academic publications, presentations, and public outreach. As a preparatory course for the senior capstone course, this course equips students with practical tools to bridge the gap between scientific research and broader audiences. Prerequisite(s): COMP 2008 and one of MATH 2233, MATH 2234, or MATH 2235, with a minimum grade of C- in both courses.
prerequisites: COMP 2008 and one of MATH 2233, MATH 2234, or MATH 2235, with a minimum grade of C- in both courses.

description: This course focuses on how peace and justice can be implemented in practice. Students will learn about the challenges and opportunities of implementing anti-racist, anti-imperial, and decolonial peace and justice in various contexts, starting with Indigenous-settler relations in what is today called Canada. Course material will explore past and ongoing cases in regions across the world, such as Africa, the Middle East, Asia, and South America. The course examines how people can collectively work together to transform structures of violence.

In this experiential course, students will investigate, discuss, critique, and apply advanced biological anthropological techniques and methods to analyze archaeological skeletal and/or dental remains. Practical components may use actual archaeological remains.
prerequisites: ANTH 3321 with a minimum grade of B- or consent of the department.

course_title: Fundamentals of Sports and Entertainment Marketing
description: Credit(s): 3 Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is a specialized course for students interested in expanding their knowledge of the sports and entertainment industry as a business and economic force. It will provide students with a framework for understanding the scope of the sports and entertainment business across the various leagues, the venues, the sponsors, the media companies, the agencies, the athletes and artists and their relationships to internal and external factors, and the marketing applications that drive this complex and growing multi-billion-dollar industry. The sports and entertainment business has become a persistent and integral part of our economy, specifically in the multi-media and entertainment arenas. The sports and entertainment business has grown significantly and is highly competitive for fans, airtime, media coverage and revenues, and sponsorship dollars. This has made managing events, venues, artists, and athletes, as well as the accountability of sponsorship and celebrity endorsement, a high priority with bottom-line relevance. Prerequisite(s): MKTG 3258
prerequisites: MKTG 3258

description: Digital Earth is a multidisciplinary field that brings together diverse knowledge and technologies to address critical environmental, social, and economic challenges facing the planet. This course provides an introduction to Digital Earth tools while introducing concepts of spatial thinking and spatial problem-solving from global to local scale across many disciplines using cloud-based GIS and virtual globe platforms.

This course provides hands-on experience with common geospatial technologies (e.g., GPS, UAV orthophotography, LiDAR, laser scanners, FieldApps and Survey 123), methods used for spatial data collection, and data preparation for integration and use in spatial data science analyses. Topics include research design, mission planning, sampling strategy, data collection, collection app building, data verification, data cleaning, and data management for remote sensing and GIS applications. Hands-on field experience will include on-the-ground situational awareness, planning and logistics, data collection, and metadata creation.
prerequisites: GEOG 2553 General Science Option

description: The course will engage students in advanced topics relating to Web and mobile GIS, including Web GIS system architecture, geospatial web services, and geoportals. Students will apply programming skills (JavaScript, Jupyter Notebooks, Python, ArcPy, and APIs) to build custom interactive web map solutions, create custom workflows and automate analysis processes. Web-based cartographic design and user experience will be explored.
prerequisites: DATA 2402 and GEOG 2553 General Science Option

course_title: Introduction to Psychology II: Career Readiness - Work Integrated Learning
description: This course introduces key skills for career and academic readiness in psychology, emphasizing the practical application of psychology concepts to real-world contexts. Students will revisit foundational topics from introductory psychology to understand their relevance in personal and professional development. Each student will also be paired with a community partner to complete a micro-internship, applying psychological concepts to real-world projects. This course bridges academic learning with hands-on application, providing students with insights into psychology’s role across various professional settings and equipping them with a toolkit of skills for future academic and career paths.
prerequisites: PSYC 1104 with a minimum grade of C or higher

course_title: International Criminal Justice Field School Part B
description: This course explores a variety of criminal justice systems, including common law and civil law systems. The selection of countries for comparison will vary from year-to-year depending on social, economic, and/or political issues. The course includes experiential learning opportunities to one or more countries outside of Canada to provide students with a sampling of justice systems for comparison. This course also has the possibility of a community service-learning designation, which is dependent on the instructor.
prerequisites: CRJS 1001 and CRJS 1003

course_title: Canadian Criminal Justice Field School Part B
description: This course explores agencies and models under Canada’s criminal justice system. Although Canada’s criminal justice system is guided by federal legislation, the application of laws varies across provinces and territories. The locations visited will vary from year-to-year depending on social, economic, and/or political issues. The course will include experiential learning opportunities that may include Indigenous content and ways of knowing. The course has the possibility of a community service-learning co-designation, which is dependent on the instructor.
prerequisites: CRJS 1001 and CRJS 1003

description: This course provides an examination of select topics designed to explore critical or emerging topics in the area of Health and Physical Education. As a variable content course, the specific topic will be announced and advertised each semester it is offered. Students may not receive credit for this course more than once.
prerequisites: HPED 2030

description: This course is designed to develop advanced knowledge and skills in spatial data science techniques and tools that will be applied to complex real-world issues through GIS mapping, analysis, and modeling. Experiential learning will introduce students to 3D and 4D mapping techniques, multitemporal visualization, machine learning, and AI applications. Students will undertake authentic research projects with a community service learning partner, building hands-on experience with the geospatial project management life cycle.
prerequisites: GEOG 2553 or GEOG 3555 ; or consent of the department Community Service Learning, General Science Option

course_title: Work Integrated Learning in Data Science
description: Students will apply their data science skills in a real-world work environment with a partner organization. They will tackle a data-related problem identified by the organization and develop a deliverable that aligns with the organization’s needs. Projects may involve tasks such as data collection, analysis, modeling, or interpretation, depending on the student’s background and the placement’s focus. Prerequisite(s): Completion of COOP 0001 and DATA 3463 with a minimum grade of C-. Note: The course will run three hours of lecture for first two weeks, with the rest of the semester being internship hours. Students will complete 60-80 Internship hours over the course of the semester. Students will have a lecture sesssion scheduled at the end of the semester for their final showcase. Back to Top | Print-Friendly Page (opens a new window)
prerequisites: Completion of COOP 0001 and DATA 3463 with a minimum grade of C-.

course_title: Design and Brand Research in Practice
description: Credit(s): 3 Lecture Hour(s): 3 Other Hour(s): 12 Other Hours Schedule Type: Experiential Learning Design researchers collaborate across development, design, and marketing teams to evaluate current and upcoming user research needs that help to improve product definition and drive business goals. In this project-based course, students will learn how to create a research plan, find participants and integrate research methods in the context of a specific branding project. Some of the topics explored will be qualitative, quantitative, online and ethnographic interview techniques, video and photo documentary, immersion, participant-aided data gathering, prototype-assisted observation, methods for organizing data, finding patterns and distilling insights that lead to actionable and inspiring design directives for the brands of the future. We will also explore ways to integrate market research and design research to create insightful and impactful branded experiences, focusing on how their combined strengths can enhance design outcomes.
prerequisites: MKTG 3258 and MKTG 3350

course_title: Community Collaboration with Children, Youth and Families
description: Credit(s): 6 Lecture Hour(s): 5, 6 Other Hour(s): 35, 5 Other Hours Schedule Type: Field Work This interdisciplinary course will focus on the development of community-based projects in a diverse socio-cultural setting where students will learn alongside children, youth, and families. Students will develop skills related to needs assessment, planning and enacting projects, and program evaluation. Students will explore theories and practices of leadership, team building, managing change and collaboration with others. Intercultural and interpersonal communication will be nurtured. The course will be delivered using experiential learning principles, acknowledged as critical for excellence in undergraduate education. Experiential learning means learning from experience or learning by doing. This approach involves immersing learners in an experience and reflecting about the experience in order to develop new skills and new ways of thinking. Experiential learning endeavours to make connections between learning and the real world and for learners may demand self-discovery, purposeful endeavour, reflection, meaningful relationships, and venturing beyond ones’ established comfort zone. Prerequisite(s): ELCC 1104 or CYCC 1132 or one of GNED 1301 or GNED 1303 , or approval of the department. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
prerequisites: ELCC 1104 or CYCC 1132 or one of GNED 1301 or GNED 1303 , or approval of the department.

description: This course examines French coloniality (French colonial past and its neocolonial present) from a cultural perspective in relation to its decolonial counter-response.
prerequisites: FREN 2250 or consent of the department

description: This course is aimed at consolidating students’ writing skills in Spanish at a high-intermediate level through the introduction of creative writing as a component of language learning. The course brings together grammar and vocabulary review, reading of short texts (fictional, testimonial, articles) and an introduction to creative writing tools. Students will explore and practice new ways of expression, such as descriptions, dialogues, and simple plots, improving their language proficiency while expanding their creativity and imagination.
prerequisites: SPAN 2213 , or two of SPAN 2223 , SPAN 2211, or SPAN 2258 ; or consent of the department.

description: This course is designed to immerse students in the expansive and diverse Francophone realms. Through this course, students will gain insights into the multifaceted economic, historical, political, social, cultural, and linguistic dimensions of Francophone nations. The curriculum aims to foster a comprehensive global perspective and a deep appreciation for the richness of various cultures.
prerequisites: FREN 2218 or consent of the department.

course_title: Introduction to Dramatic Literature and Theatre
description: This course introduces students to the foundational elements of dramatic literature, theatre, and performance. Students will experience creative work that has contributed to the world of theatre and artistic expression. They will consider play texts in a variety of contexts across time and place, journeying from tragic form in Ancient Greece to Indigenous resurgence on Turtle Island. In addition to examining plays on the page, the course explores plays on the stage and their impact on audiences. Students will gain experiential knowledge in their reception and analysis of performances ranging from full-scale productions to everyday live presentations.
prerequisites: One of ENGL 1101/GNED 1401, GNED 1403, GNED 1404, ENGL 1151, ENGL 1152, ENGL 2207, or ENGL 2263. Standard Letter

description: In this course, students will explore how youth crime is socially constructed, measured, and explained, with attention to race, gender, homelessness, and youth gangs. A strong emphasis is placed on using criminological theories to better understand youth crime and applying these theories to analyze real-world situations and policy responses. By the end of the course, students will be familiar with the Youth Criminal Justice Act, learn to use and critically assess explanations of youth crime, and appreciate the social and structural factors that lead to youth crime.
prerequisites: CRJS 2015

description: An introduction to the materials, processes, and structure of the Earth. Topics include: the classification and identification of rocks and minerals; processes in Earth’s interior; plate tectonics and mountain building; surface processes and geologic hazards; geologic resources; and geologic time.

A study of Earth’s geologic history as interpreted from sedimentary rocks. Emphasis is on rock types and fossils, and their stratigraphic and environmental significance, as they relate to the physical and organic evolution of North America from Precambrian to the recent. General Science Option, GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2

This course will seek out a fulsome understanding of the development of Métis collectivism in Canada. Students will explore both historical and contemporary events and relationships that led to Métis peoplehood and self-governance. These explorations will allow students to gain an understanding of Métis kinscapes, resistances to colonialism, identity, and nation-nation relationships.
prerequisites: INST 1101

description: This course will teach how to implement and apply machine learning algorithms. The emphasis will be on practical skills, and the course will focus on giving students skills to make these algorithms work. Students will learn about commonly used learning techniques including supervised and unsupervised learning techniques. Performance evaluation and model selection techniques will be discussed in this course. Students will gain hands-on experience through course assignments and projects.
prerequisites: MATH 2303 , MATH 3552 , DATA 3453 and DATA 3463 , each with a minimum grade of C-.

description: This course explores advanced algorithmic techniques for solving complex optimization problems. Topics include modeling and solving problems using flow networks, designing and analyzing approximation algorithms for NP-hard combinatorial problems, leveraging randomization for efficient algorithm design, and understanding the principles and performance bounds of online algorithms.
prerequisites: COMP 3614 with a minimum grade of C-

course_title: Mental Health and Addiction in Criminal Justice
description: Credit(s): 3 Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course, students will critically examine the connection between mental health, addiction, and the criminal justice system, as well as the management of drug users across social institutions. In this course, we will explore the idea that mental health and addiction cannot be understood as biological phenomena or individual issues, but instead flow from broader societal influences and contexts, from structural and cultural to political, economic and interpersonal forces. Themes considered may include : theories of drug use and drug culture, as well as perspectives on addiction and mental illness; social constructions of drug use and mental health; the relationship between mental health disorders, drug use and social disadvantage; social norms around drug use and mental health; drug policy, criminalization, and regulation, with an emphasis on the treatment of individuals with mental health and substance use disorders within the justice system. This course will empower students to think critically about issues surrounding mental health and addiction and its control. Prerequisite(s): CRJS 1001
prerequisites: CRJS 1001

description: Highlights the interdisciplinary applied science approaches to restore remnant ecosystems or to generate novel ecosystems following site decommissioning. Examines the use of ecological theory to achieve regulatory compliance and certification in closure plan implementation. Major topics include soil salvage and storage, native seed harvesting, Indigenous perspectives in land reclamation, soil reconstruction, plant reintroduction and invasive species management. Practitioner perspectives and experiences are provided by invited guest speakers.
prerequisites: ECOL 2201 and ENVS 3335

description: This course provides a systematic overview of human physiology. Topics include physiology of the nervous, circulatory, respiratory, urinary, digestive, muscular, endocrine, immune, and reproductive systems. Labs are designed to help students discover and appreciate the body’s functions.
prerequisites: BIOL 1202 with a grade of C- or higher.

description: This course provides students with an introduction to, and a critical analysis of, current practices in business Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) Sustainability reporting in Canada and worldwide. Topics covered include corporate sustainability reporting practices, sustainability regulatory requirements, reporting standards and frameworks, data sources and management, rating agencies, and investment products. The course also introduces the use of corporate sustainability disclosures in financial and valuation analyses. The class format combines lectures, interactive discussions, case studies, group projects, analysis and discussions, and project presentations.
prerequisites: ACCT 2121

description: This course provides a foundation in probabilistic thinking, with an emphasis on conceptual understanding and applications. Topics include conditional probability, Bayes’ Theorem, discrete and continuous random variables, and important probability distributions. Covariance, correlation, and moment-generating functions are also studied. Statistical software is used to bridge probability theory with simulation and applications.
prerequisites: MATH 1200 , MATH 1203 , one of MATH 2233 or MATH 2234 or MATH 2233 , and one of MATH 2071 or MATH 1271 , all with grades of C- or higher. General Science Option

description: This course explores the quantitative foundations of ecology through data, models, and uncertainty. In this course, students explore how ecological patterns and processes can be described and predicted using data and models, as well as quantitative approaches to understanding population growth and species interactions. There will be a strong emphasis on linking mathematical models to empirical data and the interpretation of these models in an ecological context, particularly as they apply to conservation and management.
prerequisites: BIOL 2213 and MATH 2233 , with grades of C- or higher General Science Option

description: Examine the complex relationships between conventional and alternative sources of energy, the environment, and human society. Emphasis is placed on historical and contemporary forms of energy (e.g., petroleum, coal, nuclear, and renewables) in terms of environmental, social, political, and economic impacts in Canadian and global contexts. Topics include: strategies for exploration and exploitation of natural resources needed for energy production and distribution, applications of energy systems, social effects of energy use, future of energy generation, and past and present economic issues. Discussion is based on interdisciplinary (humanities and natural sciences) policy choices related to the worldwide effects of energy technologies and options for transforming energy systems to reduce environmental impacts.
prerequisites: GNED 1101 , GNED 1102 , or GNED 1103 , with a grade of C- or higher. General Science Option

description: This course examines current trends in digital marketing. Students will learn how to strategically use a variety of digital marketing tools and tactics to capture consumers’ attention and achieve marketing goals. This course will explore a variety of topics such as content marketing, search engine optimization, email marketing, paid search, etc. as part of a larger digital marketing strategy.
prerequisites: MKTG 3258 and MKTG 3550

description: This course provides a deep exploration of database management for the evolving big data paradigm, and covers topics such as scalable data modeling, distributed storage and querying, and general big data technologies and processes. The course discusses big data characteristics, sources, and challenges, as well as techniques and frameworks for large-scale data ingestion and analysis, and their benefits across various industries. Students will learn foundational and practical skills for designing, building, and managing large, distributed systems handling massive, fast, and diverse data. Prerequisite(s): DATA 2721 and one of either DATA 2402 or COMP 2631 , each with a minimum grade of C-. Back to Top | Print-Friendly Page (opens a new window)
prerequisites: DATA 2721 and one of either DATA 2402 or COMP 2631 , each with a minimum grade of C-. Back to Top | Print-Friendly Page (opens a new window)

description: This course provides an overview of the estate planning process. Students will learn about the personal property interests, family law, estate law, wills, powers of attorney, taxation upon death, trusts, and estate freezes. The course also covers the financial services regulations and professional ethics. Students will critically evaluate legal and ethical aspects of applied financial cases.
prerequisites: FNCE 3227

description: This course offers a comprehensive lesson on how individuals can plan for and make informed financial decisions for their retirement. The course covers the retirement planning process, the various private and government pension plans, investment products for retirement, income tax implications, and the numerous strategies available for retirement planning.
prerequisites: FNCE 3227

description: This course builds the knowledge and judgment necessary to manage personal business and financial assets. The course examines how various business forms (including proprietorships, partnerships, and corporations) are structured and taxed, and how they can be used to generate wealth for the owner/manager or the passive investor. It covers the taxation of investment income, including property income, interest, dividends, and capital gains, and reviews the main deductions available for various income sources. Students also learn how an individual can utilize multiple tax strategies to maximize their wealth accumulation.
prerequisites: FNCE 3227

description: This course examines the theories and approaches towards strategic investment planning.
