Among the financial instruments, students will learn to analyze and evaluate different interest-producing investments, including bonds and debentures, mutual funds, and financial derivatives. The course also covers methods for fundamental and technical analysis of potential investments, as well as real estate and tax shelter opportunities.
prerequisites: FNCE 3227

description: This course aims to consolidate students’ understanding of the individual risk management process and the various insurance products available to manage financial risk. The course covers and evaluates the risk management approaches for individual disability, death, property, and liability losses. Students will also learn the legal requirements for insurance policies and government benefit plans, the application of various risk management scenarios, and the current issues confronting insurance and risk management practices.
prerequisites: FNCE 3227

description: This course examines basic beliefs concerning what is “sensible”, “right”, and “good” in promoting learning. Participants will critically reflect upon significant issues and experiences and will develop their philosophy of teaching and learning.
prerequisites: EDUC 1233 with minimum grade of B.

course_title: The Impact of Social Issues in Education and Schooling
description: This course examines factors such as the economy, the state, social class, gender, ethnicity, sub-cultural membership, ideology, religion, and the home environment, and their relationship to and impact on learning in general and schooling in particular.
prerequisites: A grade of B or higher in EDUC 1233.

description: Exploration of a topic in Computer Science at a highly specialized level typical of a 4000-level course. Topics vary from year to year and from section to section.
prerequisites: Consent of the department.

description: Exploration of a topic in Computer Science at a specialized level typical of a 3000-level course. Topics vary from year to year and from section to section.
prerequisites: Consent of the department.

description: This course develops students’ practical knowledge of lithic and ceramic technologies-the two most prevalent materials found in archaeological context. Students will spend half of the course in lectures, learning about the key concepts associated with the analysis of both technologies. The other half will be spent in the laboratory, where students will get hands-on, analytical experience with stone and ceramic artifacts, applying the concepts learned in lectures. This course prepares students for field and laboratory archaeological work.
prerequisites: ANTH 1105 and ANTH 2104

course_title: Treatment of Addictions - Work Integrated Learning
description: This course examines the history, theory, and evidence base underlying the treatment of substance use and behavioral addictions. Emphasis is placed on contemporary approaches to assessment, intervention, and recovery support across clinical and community settings. Students will explore a range of treatment modalities-including pharmacological, psychotherapeutic, behavioral, and systems-level interventions-as well as emerging models such as harm reduction, recovery-oriented care, and integrated treatment for co-occurring disorders. The course also highlights ethical, cultural, and policy considerations in delivering addiction care. As part of this course, students will collaborate with a community partner to complete a work-integrated learning experience focused on applied treatment practices.
prerequisites: PSYC 3502 with a grade of C or higher.

course_title: Practicing Anthropology: Skills for Career and Academic Success - Work-Integrated Learning
description: This course introduces key skills for career and academic readiness by emphasizing the practical application of anthropological theoretical and research concepts to real-world issues. Each student will be paired with a community partner to complete a micro-internship. This course bridges academic learning with hands-on application, providing students with insights into how anthropological knowledge can assist with their career objectives and educational goals.
prerequisites: One of ANTH 1103 or ANTH 1106 and one of ANTH 1101 or ANTH 1105 , or consent of the department.

course_title: Practicing Sociology: Skills for Career and Academic Success - Work-Integrated Learning
description: This course introduces key skills for career and academic readiness by emphasizing the practical application of sociological theoretical and research concepts to real-world issues. Each student will be paired with a community partner to complete a micro-internship. This course bridges academic learning with hands-on application, providing students with insights into how sociological knowledge can assist with their career objectives and educational goals.
prerequisites: SLGY 1101 or consent of the department.

description: This course provides students with a deeper understanding of the Canadian criminal legal system through an examination of its colonial roots. Students will gain an understanding of actus reus, mens rea , parties to a criminal offense, criminal liability, due process, and legal defenses. Selected criminal offenses will be reviewed.
prerequisites: CRJS 1003 and CRJS 1010 .

description: This course introduces students to state-Indigenous relationships in Canada. It explores how colonial state policies toward Indigenous peoples have created contemporary policy problems, and how Indigenous peoples are exercising governing authority both within and outside formal state structures to address these challenges.
prerequisites: PLSC 2231

description: This course examines power as a central framework for understanding political life in a global context. It analyzes how colonialism, capitalism, and gender shape the formation of states as institutions of coercion, regulation, and redistribution that classify and stratify subjects. Students will explore how people respond to and contest state actions, and consider the emergence, transformation, and uncertain futures of state power and political order.
prerequisites: SLGY 1101

course_title: Data-Driven Thinking: Foundations of Data and Machine Learning
description: This course introduces students to the fundamentals of data literacy, including data representation, visualization, and storytelling with data. Students will also explore how machine learning (ML) models use data to make predictions and drive decision-making. Through hands-on exercises, students will build and evaluate simple ML models using accessible tools-without requiring programming experience. Ethical considerations, data security, and the societal impact of AI will also be discussed. Prerequisite(s): One of GNED 1101 , GNED 1102 , or GNED 1103 GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 3 Back to Top | Print-Friendly Page (opens a new window)
prerequisites: One of GNED 1101 , GNED 1102 , or GNED 1103

course_title: Spanish pronunciation and spelling for communication
description: This course focuses on Spanish pronunciation and spelling in order to enhance oral and written communication skills. Its main objectives are the familiarization with the basic concepts of phonetics, and the improvement of pronunciation, intonation, accentuation and spelling. It also aims to strengthen grammar and vocabulary knowledge. SPAN 2258 has a communicative approach that brings together notions of pronunciation and spelling, and reinforcement of grammar and vocabulary, integrating the four basic skills of language learning: listening, speaking, reading and writing.
prerequisites: SPAN 1103 , or consent of the department.

description: This course introduces short film studies through a dedicated emphasis on the visual and audio techniques commonly employed by filmmakers. Topics may include but are not limited to editing styles, sound design, shot composition, temporal aesthetics, and special effects. Examples from the silent film era up to the rise of the micro short will be studied, as students work in groups to create their own short film experiments and video essays to reflect their learning.
prerequisites: One of ENGL 1101 / GNED 1401 , GNED 1403 or GNED 1404 and FILM 1143 or any 2000-level FILM course.

description: This course teaches the rudiments of craft in creative writing, including elements of poetry and fiction. Through peer critique and instructor feedback, students will acquire the skills, understanding, and practice they need to write, revise, and discuss their own original work across a range of forms and genres.
prerequisites: English Language Arts 30-1 or equivalent.

description: Credit(s): 3 Lecture Hour(s): 4 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores writing that does not fit neatly in other creative writing genre courses (i.e. fiction and poetry); it explores various emerging styles, genres, formats, media, and platforms for creative writing, which may include game-based and interactive storytelling, comics and graphic narrative, multi- and trans-media, collaborative and community-based narrative, visual poetry, etc. Through peer critique and instructor feedback, students will acquire the craft skills, understanding, and practice they need to compose, revise, and discuss their own original work.
prerequisites: One of CRWT 1118 , CRWT 1117 or CRWT 1120 .

description: This course offers instruction in the elements of writing scripts for the stage, screen, audio and/or live performance. Students will learn techniques such as script formatting, writing dialogue, plotting action, working within genres, and developing characters through regular writing exercises and the study of selected example texts. They will also have the opportunity to devise, edit and create their own short scripts which may include stage plays, screenplays, teleplays, podcasts and/or live performance pieces.
prerequisites: One of CRWT 1118 , CRWT 1117 or CRWT 1120 .

description: The course covers key historical moments in the development of animation, explores the aesthetics of various animation techniques such as cel and stop motion animation, rotoscoping, or computer generated imaging, and offers critical perspectives on the cultural significance of animated works.
prerequisites: One of ENGL 1101 / GNED 1401 , GNED 1403 , GNED 1404 , CRWT 2268 , ENGL 1151 , ENGL 1152 , ENGL 2207 , ENGL 2263 , ENGL 2273 , FILM 1143 or any 2000-level FILM course is required

description: A two-week, seminar and field-based excursion to a significant Canadian or international location focused on investigating geologic history and processes that have shaped the Earth. The course emphasizes hands-on field experience through the collection, analysis, and presentation of primary geological and/or geophysical data. Students integrate data acquired using direct field observations with technological tools to interpret geologic composition and features. The region of study may vary from year to year. Pre-trip lectures introduce geologic concepts and history relevant to the selected location. The excursion requires hiking and overnight field accommodations.
prerequisites: Consent of the department.
